chapters.title: A new dad is built to nurture, and he should trust that
chapters.text:
Becoming a parent for the first time turns your world upside down, and it can shake the bond with your partner in ways you did not expect.

After about two months, life starts to settle, and a routine takes shape. Around that point, your mind often drifts back to the sexual relationship you had with your partner before the baby came.

Most OB/GYNs suggest waiting six weeks after childbirth before having sex. If you want to start sooner, make sure you feel ready and check in with your doctor.

There are plenty of reasons why sex can feel tender for new parents. You are both lost in the baby, wondering why they are crying so often. For a woman, her body is still adjusting, and the changes can make her feel out of sorts. A man may start to see her as a mother before a partner, and the image of his own mother can creep in. The same flip can happen for her, when the man in her life starts to look like a dad instead of the partner she fell in love with.

The good news is that honest talk, time, and patience can move you through it.

There are many ways to grow close again without jumping into intercourse. Holding hands, giving a back massage, or running fingers through each other's hair are all good ways to feel physical again and ease toward more.

You and your partner do not always agree on a movie or a meal, and you will not always feel aroused at the same time.

Getting back to where you were takes time, but honesty does the heavy lifting. Plan date nights when you can find childcare, talk about your feelings, and let the spark rebuild.

The first few times can feel different from before. Take it slow, give each other room, and listen.

chapters.title: The first week of a baby's life is wild, tender, and full of adjustment
chapters.text:
The moment a baby arrives, the world tilts. You live in a bubble of love, but the bubble comes with worry and upset. For a first-time parent, male or female, the whole experience can be life-altering.

Hormones are roaring through the mother's body, and her partner wants to help but rarely knows how. The whole household is readjusting, and the recovery from childbirth can take a while to settle.

It is normal for a new father to feel swamped and helpless in those early days. At the same time, a fierce love for the new baby pours out, and you want to touch and hold them as much as you can.

Right after the birth, pause and take a deep breath. Let yourself feel the moment. Do not push the feelings aside.

Trust your gut. Whatever you feel is the natural thing to do, so go with it. You will probably not get it wrong, and if you do, you will not be far off.

Set aside the idea that women are the only ones wired to care for a baby. Plenty of research shows that fathers are just as capable. You may not be able to breastfeed, but you can care in a hundred other ways.

Sometimes the birth does not go to plan, and the experience leaves a father shaken and a little traumatized. In those moments, the man may start blaming the baby for the pain his partner went through. The child had no part in it, and they are completely innocent. The reaction is more common than people think, and it needs to be caught before it grows.

Either way, be there for your partner and look after them as best you can. Even a smooth birth can leave a big emotional mark.

chapters.title: Read your baby, and you will know what to do
chapters.text:
One of the most frustrating things about the first weeks of parenthood is that the baby does not do much. You waited nine months for them, and the main act is sleep and crying. That is normal. In the first six to eight weeks, you will not get much feedback on how you are doing.

Some fathers start to feel down at this stage, and they pull back their warmth. That is a bad habit, because the harm shows up later. Try to reset how you see it. Your baby is doing what babies do, and it has nothing to do with you as a dad. Newborns cannot express love yet, but there are signs they are bonding with you. They stop crying when you pick them up. They fall asleep in your arms or against your chest. They look up at you and hold your gaze for a stretch.

Instead of chasing feedback, give them what they need. You will start to read their signals quickly, and soon you will learn that a particular cry means they are hungry, and that fussiness shows up when they are tired. The pattern is easier to spot when you stay involved.

Newborns sleep a lot, around 14 to 17 hours a day.

Your baby is not giving you the cold shoulder, and the lack of feedback has nothing to do with their love. Set reasonable expectations for yourself, and the focus stays on the baby.

Weight can be a worry in the first week. You expect the number to go up, and the scale seems to slide the other way. That is normal. Babies can drop up to 10 percent of their birth weight in the first week, and they usually gain it back fast.

A typical newborn weighs between 5 pounds 8 ounces (about 2.5 kilograms) and 8 pounds 13 ounces (about 4 kilograms).

chapters.title: Rebuilding intimacy takes time and patience
chapters.text:
Bringing a baby home shifts everything, and the relationship with your partner can change in ways you did not expect.

Around two months in, life starts to settle and a rhythm takes hold. That is often when you start thinking about the sexual side of your relationship again.

Most OB/GYNs recommend waiting six weeks after childbirth before having sex. If you want to start sooner, check in with your doctor and make sure you feel ready.

Sex can be a tender topic for new parents for many reasons. You are both focused on the baby, and you may worry about why they are crying so much. For a woman, the changes in her body can throw off how she feels. A man may start to see her as a mother before a partner, and the image of his own mother can drift in. The same can happen for her, when the man in her life starts to feel like a dad rather than the partner she fell for.

The good news is that honest talk, listening, time, and a little perspective can pull you through.

There are many ways to grow close again without jumping into intercourse. Holding hands, giving a back massage, or running fingers through each other's hair are all good ways to feel physical again and slowly build up.

You and your partner do not always agree on a movie or a meal, and you will not always feel aroused at the same time.

Getting back to where you were takes time, and honesty is the through line. Plan date nights when you can find childcare, talk about your feelings, and let the spark rebuild.

The first few times will probably feel different from before. Take it slow, give each other room, and listen.

chapters.title: A home that balances work and family is the heart of a happy life
chapters.text:
As the baby grows, your view of everything shifts, and that includes your job. Once you become a father, your focus pulls away from yourself and toward the family. The change will likely make you think about your work.

The hard truth is that employers are usually far more generous with time off for women than for men, and the workplace has not kept up with how families actually look. That gap leaves many men wrestling to blend work and home.

Look for roles that let you work from home while you find a balance. Try to arrange time off on weekends so you can be with the family. Some fathers choose to work part time to stay closer to home.

There is no clean answer to the imbalance at work yet, but you can talk with your employer and stay as flexible as you can.

Many men worry that life will never be the same as it was before the baby came. That is true, but the new version is not worse, it is just different.

Fatherhood changes you for good, and the day you became a dad often becomes a marker you cannot ignore. The author, for one, can recall his pre-fatherhood days, but his identity is so tied to being a dad that those years feel half-written. A lot of fathers feel the same. You can still connect with the past by spending time with old friends and picking up the hobbies you used to love.

For some men, fatherhood is a chance to rethink what masculinity means. Society tells you to be strong and to know everything, and the moment you falter, you feel weak. That is far from true. The day a child arrives, you will feel helpless and clueless at times. Lean into that feeling, because it teaches you a great deal about yourself and about your strengths as a father.

chapters.title: Settling into the role makes everything a little easier
chapters.text:
Life with a newborn is a little wild, but around six months, things start to calm down and a routine takes shape that works for everyone. At that point, the baby becomes much better at telling you what they want and how they feel. They still cannot talk, though, so getting clear feedback is tricky.

Fathers are often expected to teach, but how do you teach a child who does not understand the words yet? You may feel stuck in a kind of limbo, not entirely settled in the role. Even so, the signs are there. They look up when you call their name. They smile at you. They are listening, and they are taking their cues from you. You will feel more sure of yourself once you start to see those signs.

After six months, the baby will start to crawl. Tie down anything you do not want them to grab.

Still, one emotion can undo the work you have put in, and that is jealousy.

You may feel jealous of the close bond between your partner and the baby, and you may worry that there is no room for you. You may also feel jealous of the carefree life your child is living while you carry the worry. Those thoughts are destructive, and they are not worth chasing. They are more common than you might think.

Sit down and name what is fueling the jealousy. Once you can see it, you can work through it. Ask yourself whether the feeling shows up more when you are with the baby or when you are apart. The answer is a clue about where to focus.

Whichever direction the jealousy points, the answer is to speak your feelings out loud and to invite your partner to do the same.

The good news is that the strain in your relationship should start to fade around this stage. You are a family now, and you have found a rhythm that works.

chapters.title: Closing thoughts
chapters.text:
Whether you are a mother or a father, life as a new parent is confusing, emotional, sometimes tricky, and exhausting, but it is also full of joy. A lot of parenting books and programs focus on the mother and assume that women are the more nurturing sex. That is not true. Men can be caring and tender, too. The point is to grab the chance and stay as involved as you can.

Knowing the milestones your baby is likely to hit at one month, two months, three months, and beyond can give you a steady sense of confidence and bring real joy as you watch them grow. Talking to other new dads and getting support can help, too. You will quickly learn that you are not the only one feeling this way, and that everything you are going through is normal.

A lot of new fathers feel fear. Fear that they are not doing what they should, or that they are doing something wrong. Fear that they will hurt the baby by accident. Fear that they will not be able to protect their child from the world one day. That fear is normal, and it is a sign of how much you love the baby.

Fear lives in the head, not in the room. Push the dark thoughts aside and focus on the bundle of joy in front of you. When you and your partner act as a real team, you can meet the baby's changing needs and enjoy the whole ride of parenthood.

Try this

Look online for new-dad groups in your area, and join one. Open up to your partner about how you feel. They are probably wondering what is going on in your head, and you are not a burden. Read up on childhood development so you know what to expect. Knowledge gives you an edge.

Read it till the end?
